  "col_map": [
    {"target": "Death_Prob", "sources": ["Pre-Retirement Men", "Pre-Retirement Women", "Post-Retirement Men", "Post-Retirement Women"], "op": "group_weighted", "weights_tables": [1, 1, 2, 2]}
  ],
  "derive": null,
  "transpose": false,
  "notes": "Sample mortality rates from Exhibit page G-4 (page 48), projected to 2019, printed as percentages (values_unit=percent). Rates are printed sex-split at 5-year sample ages for three populations; each sample age stands for its 5-year band (row_spans). Post-Disability columns transcribed as archived evidence but EXCLUDED from the blend (disability handled separately). The blend is population-weighted between pre-retirement (weighted by active-member counts, source_table 1) and post-retirement (weighted by retiree+disabled+beneficiary counts, source_table 2). Using the same headcount table for a population's Men and Women columns yields their simple average, then actives-vs-retirees headcounts weight the two populations by age. Active counts (Exhibit F.3, Total column) use row_spans; 'Under 20' clipped to [null,19] and 'Over 65' clipped to [65,69] since active membership beyond 69 is negligible and leaving the bin open would smear its weight. Retiree weight table built by summing the three count columns of Exhibit F.6 (Service Retirees 168/594/1178/1499/1193/689/390/186/116 + Disabled 39/39/53/53/24/14/15/7/1 + Beneficiaries 73/44/58/122/134/148/139/141/78) per age band; '<55' clipped to [null,54] and '90 & Up' clipped to [90,null]. Below age 55 retirees exist (168 in <55 band per F.6) so the blend at ages 20-54 uses both populations where actives dominate. Adjustment factors (Non-Annuitant Male 1.054/Female 1.112; Healthy Annuitant Male 1.019/Female 1.061; Disabled Male 0.984/Female 1.038) and generational MP-2015 improvement are NOT included in the printed sample rates and are recorded here only as context. Target ages 95-119 exceed the last printed sample band (90-94) so they get empty sources - no extrapolation."
}
